bee_quiz.question: The science word for a group of things that share something in common is what?
In memory science, a 'chunk' best describes what?

choices.b: a group of related items remembered together

bee_quiz.question: An animal that is warm-blooded, has fur or hair, gives birth to live babies, and feeds them milk is called a what?
Apples, bananas, and grapes are sweet and grow on plants — they are all examples of which category?
A librarian who organises her books by their colour instead of by topic has chosen the wrong what?
The chapter says the human brain can comfortably hold a number of chunks roughly between what range?
A whale lives in the ocean and looks like a fish, but it is best classified as a member of which group?
bee_quiz.misconception_targeted: Whales look fish-like, so kids assume they're fish — but the four mammal traits (warm-blooded, fur, live birth, milk) classify them with dogs.

bee_quiz.question: How do scientists actually decide which category an animal belongs to?

choices.c: by hidden shared rules like body traits

bee_quiz.misconception_targeted: Categories are based on looks — kids assume looks decide groups, but it's hidden traits like fur, warm-blood, etc.

bee_quiz.question: Mia turns 18 random items into 3 grouped categories and now remembers them all. What changed in her brain?

choices.c: only how the same information was organised

bee_quiz.misconception_targeted: Better memory = more info learned. Actually, organising changes how existing info is held, not how much you know.

bee_quiz.question: When scientists meet a new oddball animal, the FIRST option the chapter lists for sorting it is to do what?

choices.a: name it after the closest existing animal
choices.b: put it in the category with the MOST features in common
choices.d: ignore it until more is known

bee_quiz.question: Of these chunk counts — 3, 7, and 18 — which is hardest for the brain to hold?
When scientists first saw a platypus, before they figured out its category, they did what?

choices.a: thought someone was playing a trick on them
choices.b: called it a brand new kingdom
choices.d: immediately knew it was a mammal

bee_quiz.question: Which of the following is **NOT** one of the four hidden traits all mammals share?
Which of these is **NOT** a benefit of organising things into categories, according to the chapter?

choices.a: makes a system look pretty no matter what

bee_quiz.question: Which of these is **NOT** something a category must have, according to the chapter?

choices.b: a similarity or purpose shared by the items

word_cards.definition: A group of things put together because they all share something the same.
word_cards.example: Apples, bananas, and grapes belong together as 'fruits' because they all grow on plants and taste sweet.

word_cards.definition: A small group of related ideas or items that your brain holds together as one single piece.
word_cards.example: Remembering the four numbers 1-9-8-4 as just '1984 — the year my dad was born' is one of these.

word_cards.definition: An animal that is warm-blooded, has fur or hair, gives birth to live babies, and feeds those babies milk.
word_cards.example: A dog, a whale, a bat, and you all belong to this group — even though you all look very different.

word_cards.definition: Something that repeats in a way you can notice — the same shape, colour, or rule showing up again and again.
word_cards.example: Stripes on a tiger, the way Mondays always feel tired, or how rain often follows dark clouds.

word_cards.definition: The reason something is being done — what you are trying to use it for.
word_cards.example: A library is sorted by topic, not by colour, because the reason people come is to find books about a subject.

word_cards.definition: Something two or more things have in common — a way they are the same.
word_cards.example: A cat and a dog are not the same animal, but they share that both have fur and four legs.

word_cards.definition: A thing that doesn't fit neatly into the groups you already have.
word_cards.example: A tomato is a famous one — it grows like a fruit but tastes like a vegetable, so people argue where it goes.

word_cards.definition: To put a pile of things into separate groups based on a rule you choose.
word_cards.example: At the supermarket, milk, cheese, and butter all go on the dairy shelf — that's a rule about what they are.

word_cards.definition: An animal that is cold-blooded, has scales, and usually lays eggs.
word_cards.example: A snake, a lizard, a turtle, and a crocodile are all in this group.

word_cards.definition: The process of looking at a bunch of items and deciding which groups they belong in.
word_cards.example: When a librarian decides which shelf each new book belongs on, that's what she is doing.

v2_changes: Q4: swapped 'dogs/dolphins/whales -> mammals' for 'apples/bananas/grapes -> fruits' to reduce mammal over-rep while keeping example-to-category framing
Q5: swapped 'minimum 2 things to make a category' for 'librarian sorts by colour -> wrong purpose' to address purpose-coverage gap
Q9: shuffled positions so correct moved from b to c (rebalance)
Q10: reframed from chunk-count drop (recall mislabel) to oddball-options ordering (true sequence/list-position question)
Q13/Q14/Q15: bolded the negative marker (**NOT**) for visual emphasis
Categorisation card: definition rewritten to avoid 'putting them into groups' echo of the word being defined
_self_check.instrument_or_unit_reason: Chapter has no measurement instruments or SI units. Brief mention of '15 seconds' and '4-7 chunks' but these are facts, not instruction in measurement tools.
